elements.sent-2411-001: We study the entanglement entropy in quasiparticle states where certain unit patterns are excited repeatedly and sequentially in momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-002: We find that in the scaling limit, each unit pattern contributes independently and universally to the entanglement, leading to a volume-law scaling of the entanglement entropy.
elements.sent-2411-003: This characteristic of volume-law entanglement fragmentation is numerically confirmed in both fermionic and bosonic chains.
elements.sent-2411-004: We derive an analytical formula for fermions, which can also be applied to the spin-1/2 XXZ chain with appropriate identifications.
elements.sent-2411-005: Quantum entanglement has been instrumental in various areas of physics, such as quantum information theory, condensed matter physics, and high-energy physics \cite{Amico:2007ag,Eisert:2008ur,Calabrese:2009bph,Laflorencie:2015eck,Rangamani:2016dms,Witten:2018lha .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Notably, entanglement adheres to distinct scaling laws in various phases of quantum systems, which allows it to serve as an indicator of quantum phase transitions \cite{Osterloh:2002auc,Osborne:2002zz .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In the context of one-dimensional quantum spin chains with energy gaps, the ground state entanglement entropy typically conforms to the area law.
elements.sent-2411-008: Conversely, in critical spin chains, the ground state entropy adheres to a logarithmic law, with the proportionality constant being related to the central charge \cite{Holzhey:1994we,Vidal:2002rm,Latorre:2003kg,Korepin:2004zz,Calabrese:2004eu .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The study of the time evolution of entanglement entropy following a quantum quench in many-body systems \cite{Calabrese:2005in has motivated research into entanglement entropy in excited states.}
elements.sent-2411-010: This includes both low-energy states \cite{Alba:2009th,Alcaraz:2011tn,Berganza:2011mh,Pizorn:2012aut,Essler:2012rai,Berkovits:2013mii,Storms:2013wzf,Calabrese:2014ntv, Molter:2014qsb,Taddia:2016dbm,Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja,Castro-Alvaredo:2018bij,Castro-Alvaredo:2019irt,Castro-Alvaredo:2019lmj, Jafarizadeh:2019xxc,You:2020osa,Zhang:2020vtc,Zhang:2020dtd,Zhang:2021bmy , where the entropy adheres to either the area law or logarithmic law, and high-energy states \cite{Deutsch:2012vkm,Santos:2012rss,Beugeling:2015plv,Garrison:2015lva,Nakagawa:2017yiw,Lu:2017tbo,Miao:2019xpp , where the entropy typically aligns with the volume law.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In \cite{Vidmar:2017uux,Vidmar:2017pak,Vidmar:2018rqk,Huang:2019dxk,Lydzba:2020qfx,Bianchi:2021aui , the average entanglement entropy across the spectrum has been employed to characterize the universal properties of chaotic and integrable systems.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Entanglement of excited states in integrable models can often be understood in terms of quasiparticles \cite{Calabrese:2005in,Alba:2017hlc,Alba:2017lvc .}
elements.sent-2411-013: When a finite number of quasiparticles with large energy and significant momentum differences are excited, a semiclassical picture can describe the entanglement entropy \cite{Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja,Castro-Alvaredo:2018bij,Castro-Alvaredo:2019irt,Castro-Alvaredo:2019lmj .}
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, when the momentum differences between the excited quasiparticles are small, there are strong coherence effects among the quasiparticles, which results in significant corrections to the semiclassical picture of the entanglement entropy \cite{Zhang:2020vtc,Zhang:2020dtd,Zhang:2021bmy .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we study the entanglement entropy in states with a large number of quasiparticles, where certain unit patterns are excited sequentially and repeatedly in the momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-017: We consider a subsystem consisting of a consecutive block within circular quantum systems, including both free and interacting fermionic and bosonic chains, as well as spin-1/2 XXZ chains.
elements.sent-2411-018: Generally, the entanglement entropy in such states follows a volume law.
elements.sent-2411-019: In \cite{Alba:2009th , analytical expressions were obtained for fermionic chains in cases where the subsystem size is much smaller than the size of the entire system.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In the case of free fermionic chains, we obtain analytical expressions for subsystems of arbitrary sizes, which, in the scaling limit, also apply to interacting fermionic chains and spin-1/2 XXZ chains.
elements.sent-2411-021: We discover that the coherence among quasiparticles enables the entanglement entropy to be decomposed into separate contributions from distinct parts, each comprising a few sites in the coordinate space and the corresponding unit pattern quasiparticles in the momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-022: In essence, the volume-law entanglement entropy breaks down into components that correspond to the quasiparticle entanglement entropy of much smaller systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: This universal phenomenon of volume-law entanglement fragmentation could be useful for establishing robust entanglement, which is crucial for applications in quantum information processing and quantum computation.
elements.sent-2411-024: A chain of $L$ free fermions has the Hamiltonian \be H = \sum_{j=1 ^L \Big( a_j^\dag a_j - \frac{1 {2 \Big), \ee with the fermionic modes $a_j$ and $a_j^\dag$ satisfying $\{a_{j_1 ,a_{j_2}\}=\{a^\dag_{j_1 ,a^\dag_{j_2}\}=0$ and $\{a_{j_1 ,a^\dag_{j_2}\}=\delta_{j_1j_2}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The excited states with translational invariance are generated by the global modes \be b_k^\dag = \frac{1 {\sqrt{L \sum_{j=1 ^L a_j^\dag \ep^{\frac{2\pi \ii j k {L , ~~ b_k = \frac{1 {\sqrt{L \sum_{j=1 ^L a_j \ep^{-\frac{2\pi \ii j k {L , \ee with the momentum $k = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We use the set of excited modes $K = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots\}$ to denote the corresponding excited energy eigenstate $|K\rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-027: For a subsystem $A$ of $L_A$ consecutive sites in a chain of total length $L$ in state $|K\rangle$, one has the reduced density matrix (RDM) $\rho_{A,K = \tr_{\bar{A (|K\rangle \langle K|)$, from which one gets the R\'enyi entropy and entanglement entropy \begin{equation}S_{L,L_A,K}^{(n)} = - \frac{1}{n-1} \log \tr_A (\rho_{A,K}^n), \nonumber S_{L,L_A,K} = - \tr_A (\rho_{A,K} \log \rho_{A,K}).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The entanglement entropy is just the $n \to 1$ limit of the R\'enyi entropy $S_{L,L_A,K ^{(1) = S_{L,L_A,K}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the free fermionic chain, one has \cite{Cheong:2002ukf,Vidal:2002rm,Peschel:2002jhw,Latorre:2003kg \begin{equation}S_{L,L_A,K}^{(n),fer} = - \frac{1}{n-1} \log \det[ C_{L,L_A,K}^n + (1 - C_{L,L_A,K})^n ], \nonumber S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A [ - C_{L,L_A,K} \log C_{L,L_A,K} \nonumber \phantom{S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A} - (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) \log (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) ],\end{equation} where the $L_A \times L_A$ matrix has entries \begin{equation}[C_{L,L_A,K}]_{j_1j_2} = \langle a_{j_1}^\dag a_{j_2} \rangle_K = h_{L,j_2-j_1,K},\end{equation} with $j_1, j_2 = 1, 2, \cdots, L_A$ and the correlation function \begin{equation}\label{hLjK} h_{L,j,K} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k \in K} \ep^{\frac{2\pi \ii j k}{L}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}S_{L,L_A,K}^{(n),fer} = - \frac{1}{n-1} \log \det[ C_{L,L_A,K}^n + (1 - C_{L,L_A,K})^n ], \nonumber S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A [ - C_{L,L_A,K} \log C_{L,L_A,K} \nonumber \phantom{S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A} - (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) \log (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) ],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: We first consider the case that $K$ is composed of the repetition of a unit pattern in the whole momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-032: The unit pattern has $l$ consecutive sites in the momentum space, in which the excited modes are $\k$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The whole system has $L=pl$ sites, and we denote the excited modes of the whole momentum space as $K$ and use the shorthand $K = p\k$.
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance, there are states \bea l=2, ~ \k=\{0\}, ~ p=4: ~ K=\{ 0 , 2, 4, 6 \}, \nn l=3, ~ \k=\{0,1\}, ~ p=2: ~ K=\{0,1,3,4\}, \eea which can be denoted schematically as \be \bullet\!\circ\!\bullet\!\circ\!\bullet\!\circ\!\bullet\!\circ, ~~ \bullet\!\bullet\!\circ\!\bullet\!\bullet\!\circ, \ee with the filled circles denoting the excited modes and the empty circles denoting the modes that are not excited.
elements.sent-2411-036: From (\ref{hLjK ) we get the correlation function \be h_{pl,j,p\k = \delta_{(j\,{\rm mod}\,p)=0 h_{l,j/p,\k , \ee which is possibly nonvanishing only when $j$ is an integer multiple of $p$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The $L \times L$ correlation matrix of the whole system $C_{L,L,K}$ can be written as a matrix with $l \times l$ blocks, with each block being proportional to the $p \times p$ identity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-038: The $L_A \times L_A$ correlation matrix $C_{L,L_A,K}$ for the $L_A$-sized subsystem is just the first $L_A$ rows and the first $L_A$ columns of $C_{L,L,K}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: We parameterize the subsystem length as $L_A=\a p+a$, with $\a=0,1,\cdots,l-1$ and $a=0,1,\cdots,p-1$, and the corresponding correlation matrix $C_{L,L_A,K}$ is similar to \be C_{pl,\a p+a,p\k \sim \underbrace{ C_{l,\a+1,\k}\oplus\cdots _{a \oplus \underbrace{ C_{l,\a,\k}\oplus\cdots _{p-a .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The subsystem has $L_A=\a p+a$ sites, and they are effectively composed of subsystems of the $p$ copies of the small systems.
elements.sent-2411-041: An example scheme of the entanglement fragmentation is shown in Fig.~\ref{FigureFragmentation .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.19\textwidth]{FigureFragmentation.pdf} \caption{Example scheme of the entanglement fragmentation. Each pair of concentric circles symbolizes a system. The larger circle represents the spatial configuration, where red and blue points denote the complementary subsystems. The smaller circle depicts the momentum configuration, with filled purple points indicating excited modes and hollow points representing unexcited modes. For this illustration, we have set the parameters to $L=15$, $L_A=7$, $\alpha=1$, $a=2$, $p=5$, $l=3$, and $\k=\{0\}$.}\label{FigureFragmentation}
elements.sent-2411-042: From the RDM (\ref{rplappapk ), we get the decomposition of the R\'enyi entropy and entanglement entropy \be S_{pl,\a p+a,p\k ^{(n),\fer = a S_{l,\a+1,\k ^{(n),\fer + (p-a) S_{l,\a,\k ^{(n),\fer .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Explicitly, we have \be s^{(n),\fer _{l,\k (x) = y S^{(n),\fer _{l,\a+1,\k + ( 1 - y ) S^{(n),\fer _{l,\a,\k , \ee with the parameters \bea \a \equiv \lfloor l x \rfloor \in \{ 0,1,\cdots,l-1 \}, \nn y \equiv (l x{\rm\,mod\, 1) \in [0,1).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, the function $s_{l,\k ^{(n),\fer (x)$ is independent of $p$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For finite $l$ and large $p$, i.e.\ $l\ll p$, the R\'enyi entropy and entanglement entropy (\ref{Spnapapkn ) follow the volume law.}

elements.sent-2411-047: We use $|\k|$ to denote the number of excited quasiparticles in the set $\k$.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the special limit, $|\k|\ll l$, the formula (\ref{snferlkx ) becomes \be \lim_{l\to+\inf s^{(n),\fer _{l,\k (x) = \lim_{l\to+\inf S_{l,xl,\k ^{(n),\fer .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For $\k=\{0\}$, with $l=2,3,\cdots$, there are \cite{Pizorn:2012aut,Berkovits:2013mii,Molter:2014qsb,Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja,Castro-Alvaredo:2018bij \bea S_{l,l_A,\{0\} ^{(n),\fer = - \f{1 {n-1 \log \Big[ \Big(\f{l_A {l}\Big)^n + \Big(1-\f{l_A {l}\Big)^n \Big], \nn S_{l,l_A,\{0\} ^{\fer = - \f{l_A {l}\log\f{l_A {l - \Big(1-\f{l_A {l}\Big)\log\Big(1-\f{l_A {l}\Big), \eea with which the formula (\ref{Spnapapkn ) is the same as the comb entropy defined in \cite{Keating:2006psq after one takes the position-momentum duality \cite{Lee:2014nra,Carrasco:2017eul .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-052: In the excited states, the unit pattern $\k$ of length $l$ can be repeated $p$ times in the momentum space and these repeated patterns only occupy a finite ratio of the whole momentum space, leaving the rest of the momentum space unoccupied.
elements.sent-2411-053: We call such state partially occupied states.

elements.sent-2411-056: We also consider more general states, in which different unit patterns are excited in different parts of the momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-057: The parts with different unit patterns may be adjacent or disjoint in the momentum space, and all the excited parts together may or may not occupy the entire spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-058: Since the relative positions of the different patterns are not important, we denote such states shorthand as $K = \bigcup_{i=1 ^r (p_i \k_i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Each excited part is characterized by the length of the unit pattern $ l_i $, the mode of the unit pattern $ \k_i $, and the number of repetitions $ p_i $.
elements.sent-2411-060: In the scaling limit, we define \be z_i \equiv \lim_{L \to +\infty \frac{p_i {L .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, we may have the unit pattern with $l_1 = 2$ and $\k_1 = \{0\}$ excited in the momentum space $[0, \frac{L {3 - 1]$, and the unit pattern with $l_2 = 3$ and $\k_2 = \{0, 1\}$ excited in the momentum space $[\frac{L {2 , \frac{3L {4 - 1]$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-063: We have expressed the volume-law many-body entanglement entropy as the sum of few-body entanglement entropies.

elements.sent-2411-067: By applying the Jordan-Wigner transformation, the transverse field Ising chain, given by \be H = -\frac{1 {2 \sum_{j=1 ^L \big( \sigma_j^x \sigma_{j+1 ^x + h \sigma_j^z \big), \ee is transformed into a fermionic chain with nearest-neighbor interactions.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-069: We have carried out extensive numerical verifications of formulas (\ref{theformulasIII ) and (\ref{theformulasIV ) in the Ising chain, thereby confirming the aforementioned conjecture.}}

elements.sent-2411-082: Similarly to (\ref{theformulasIV ) in the fermionic chain, we conjecture that in the free and interacting bosonic chains, there is \be \lim_{L \to +\infty \frac{S_{L,xL,\bigcup_{i=1 ^r(p_i\k_i) ^{(n),\text{bos {L = \sum_{i=1 ^r z_i s_{l_i,\k_i ^{(n),\text{bos (x), \ee with the definition of $z_i$ in (\ref{zi ) and the definition of $s_{l_i,\k_i ^{(n),\text{bos (x)$ in (\ref{slikinbosx ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-084: The harmonic chain is essentially a chain of bosons with nearest-neighbor interactions.
elements.sent-2411-085: The above conjecture could be easily checked by numerically calculating the R\'enyi entropy in excited energy eigenstates following the wavefunction method \cite{Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja,Castro-Alvaredo:2018bij,Zhang:2020ouz,Zhang:2020txb .}
elements.sent-2411-086: We have studied the entanglement entropy in quasiparticle states that exhibit repeated and sequential unit patterns in momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-087: We found that each unit pattern contributes independently and universally to the entanglement in the scaling limit, leading to a volume-law scaling of entanglement entropy.
elements.sent-2411-088: Numerical simulations confirmed these findings, demonstrating volume-law entanglement fragmentation in both fermionic and bosonic chains.
elements.sent-2411-089: We derived an analytical formula for free fermions that is applicable not only to interacting fermions in the scaling limit but also, with certain identifications, to the spin-1/2 XXZ chain.
elements.sent-2411-090: It was suggested in \cite{Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja to harness the entanglement of quasiparticles for quantum information purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-091: However, the entanglement among a small number of quasiparticles is delicate and susceptible to environmental disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-092: The results presented in this paper imply that by successively exciting repeated patterns in momentum space, it is possible to create a robust volume-law entanglement of quasiparticles.
elements.sent-2411-093: The author thanks Olalla A.. Castro-Alvaredo and M.~A.
elements.sent-2411-094: Rajabpour for reading a previous versions of the draft and helpful comments and discussions.
elements.sent-2411-095: JZ acknowledgements support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) grant number 12205217.. \providecommand{\href [2]{#2}\begingroup \endgroup.}
elements.sent-2411-096: We investigate the entanglement entropy in quantum states featuring repeated sequential excitations of unit patterns in momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-097: In the scaling limit, each unit pattern contributes independently and universally to the entanglement entropy, leading to a characteristic volume-law scaling.
elements.sent-2411-098: Crucially, this universal contribution remains identical for both free and interacting models, enabling decomposition of the total entanglement into pattern-specific components.
elements.sent-2411-099: Numerical verification in fermionic and bosonic chains confirms this volume-law fragmentation phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-100: For fermionic systems, we derive analytical expressions where many-body entanglement becomes expressible through few-body entanglement components.
elements.sent-2411-101: Notably, this analytical framework extends to spin-1/2 XXZ chains through appropriate identifications.
elements.sent-2411-102: This includes both low-energy states \cite{Alba:2009th,Alcaraz:2011tn,Berganza:2011mh,Pizorn:2012aut,Essler:2012rai,Berkovits:2013mii,Storms:2013wzf,Calabrese:2014ntv, Molter:2014qsb,Taddia:2016dbm,Castro-Alvaredo:2018dja,Castro-Alvaredo:2018bij,Castro-Alvaredo:2019irt,Castro-Alvaredo:2019lmj, Jafarizadeh:2019xxc,You:2020osa,Zhang:2020vtc,Zhang:2020dtd,Zhang:2021bmy , where the entropy adheres to either the area law or logarithmic law, and high-energy states \cite{Deutsch:2012vkm,Santos:2012rss,Beugeling:2015plv,Garrison:2015lva,Nakagawa:2017yiw,Lu:2017tbo,Miao:2019xpp,Mohapatra:2024jrb , where the entropy typically aligns with the volume law.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: In the remaining part of the paper, we consider free and interacting fermions in sections~\ref{secfreefer and \ref{secinterfer , spin-1/2 XXZ chain in section~\ref{secXXZ , and free and interacting bosons in sections~\ref{secfreebos and \ref{secinterbos .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We conclude with discussions in section~\ref{secdis .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The Hamiltonian is already in diagonal form, so we can derive the following analytical expressions.
elements.sent-2411-106: In the free fermionic chain, one has \cite{Cheong:2002ukf,Vidal:2002rm,Peschel:2002jhw,Latorre:2003kg \begin{equation}S_{L,L_A,K}^{(n),fer} = - \frac{1}{n-1} \log \det[ C_{L,L_A,K}^n + (1 - C_{L,L_A,K})^n ], \nonumber S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A [ - C_{L,L_A,K} \log C_{L,L_A,K} - (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) \log (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) ],\end{equation} which applies to any Hamiltonian for which the number of excitations is conserved, with the Hamiltonian (\ref{Hfree ) as a specific example.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}S_{L,L_A,K}^{(n),fer} = - \frac{1}{n-1} \log \det[ C_{L,L_A,K}^n + (1 - C_{L,L_A,K})^n ], \nonumber S_{L,L_A,K}^{fer} = \tr_A [ - C_{L,L_A,K} \log C_{L,L_A,K} - (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) \log (1 - C_{L,L_A,K}) ],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-107: The $L_A \times L_A$ matrix $C_{L,L_A,K}$ has entries \begin{equation}[C_{L,L_A,K}]_{j_1j_2} = \langle a_{j_1}^\dag a_{j_2} \rangle_K = h_{L,j_2-j_1,K},\end{equation} with $j_1, j_2 = 1, 2, \cdots, L_A$ and the correlation function \begin{equation}\label{hLjK} h_{L,j,K} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k \in K} \ep^{\frac{2\pi \ii j k}{L}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: From (\ref{hLjK ) we get the correlation function \be h_{pl,j,p\k = \delta_{j\,{\rm mod}\,p,0 h_{l,j/p,\k , \ee which is possibly nonvanishing only when $j$ is an integer multiple of $p$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We parameterize the subsystem length as $L_A=\a p+a$, with $\a=0,1,\cdots,l-1$ and $a=0,1,\cdots,p-1$, and the corresponding correlation matrix $C_{L,L_A,K}$ can be transformed to the following form via similarity transformation \be C_{pl,\a p+a,p\k \sim \underbrace{ C_{l,\a+1,\k}\oplus\cdots\oplus C_{l,\a+1,\k _{a \oplus \underbrace{ C_{l,\a,\k}\oplus\cdots\oplus C_{l,\a,\k _{p-a .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The subsystem has $L_A=\a p+a$ sites, and it effectively decomposes into subsystems across $p$ copies of small systems.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{FigureFragmentation.pdf} \caption{Example scheme of the entanglement fragmentation. Each pair of concentric circles symbolizes a system. The larger circle represents the spatial configuration, where red and blue points denote the complementary subsystems. The smaller circle depicts the momentum configuration, with filled purple points indicating excited modes and hollow points representing unexcited modes. For this illustration, we have set the parameters to $L=15$, $L_A=7$, $\alpha=1$, $a=2$, $p=5$, $l=3$, and $\k=\{0\}$.}\label{FigureFragmentation}
elements.sent-2411-111: This inspired us to define the contributions to the entanglement from each unit pattern as \be s^{(n),\fer _{l,\k (x) \equiv \f{S^{(n),\fer _{p l,x p l,p \k {p , \ee which is the building block for all the results in the paper.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Note that these formulas are exact, valid for any $p$, $l$, $\a$, $a$, and $\k$.

elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{FigureInteractingBosons.pdf} \caption{The many-body entanglement entropy in interacting bosons (symbols) and the results $s_{l_1,\k_1}^{(2)}(x)$ and $z s_{l_1,\k_1}^{(2)}(x)$ (blue lines) with $l_1=2,\k_1=\{0\}$ and $s_{l,\k}^{(2)}(x)$ taking the approximate value from the interpolation in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{FigureFreeBosons}. elements.sent-2411-133: Numerical results of free and interacting bosonic chains reveal universal entanglement characteristics in bosonic systems; however, explicit analytical formulations remain elusive.
elements.sent-2411-134: Deriving such analytical expressions for bosonic chains would be an important direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-135: The results in this paper reveal a fundamental distinction between fragmented volume-law entanglement and thermal volume-law entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-136: Thermal volume-law entanglement, found in states such as random states or eigenstates of chaotic Hamiltonians \cite{Garrison:2015lva,Nakagawa:2017yiw,Bianchi:2021aui , emerges from maximal scrambling and adherence to the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH), resulting in featureless, homogeneous entanglement devoid of substructure.}
elements.sent-2411-137: In contrast, fragmented volume-law entanglement in quasiparticle states arises from repeated unit patterns in momentum space and decomposes the state into a sum of few-body entangled components.
elements.sent-2411-138: Although both types exhibit extensive entanglement, fragmented states violate ETH due to the underlying integrability, contrasting sharply with the thermal case.
elements.sent-2411-139: The existence and universality of entanglement fragmentation remain to be further studied.
elements.sent-2411-140: We conjecture that this phenomenon may stem from the existence and independence of quasiparticles resulting from integrability.
elements.sent-2411-141: The universality of fragmentation suggests potential for robust entanglement, though stability to environmental perturbations requires further study.
